HAZARDOUS WASTE
HEINI LASSILA
RESEARCH TECHNICIAN,
OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY REPRESENTATIVE,
CHIEF SHOP STEWARD (JUKO)

HAZARDOUS WASTE
Hazardous waste is waste that has hazard property.
The hazard statements and information on hazard classes for
chemicals can be found, for example, in section 2.1 of the safety
data sheet (SDS).
Examples of hazardous waste:
- Expired and contaminated chemicals
- Analysis waste, synthesis or reaction products
- Old and unused reagents
- Products contaminated with hazardous substances, such as
pipette tips, empty packaging
2

CLP HAZARD PICTOGRAMS
GHS01 – Explosive
 GHS02 – Flammable
 GHS03 – Oxidising
GHS08 – Serious
Health hazard3GHS04 – Gas under
pressure
GHS07 – Health hazard GHS05 – CorrosiveGHS06 – Acute
toxicity
GHS09 – Hazard to
the environment

WASTE HAZARD PROPERTIES, HP
Annex III of the Waste Framework Directive ( WFD )
lists the characteristics that make waste
hazardous, as well as the criteria (concentration
limits) used to assess these characteristics (EU
Regulation 1357/2014, Council Regulation
2017/997).
Waste is classified as hazardous waste if it has
even one hazard property defined in the
Commission regulation!
Source:
Commission notice on technical guidance on the classification
of waste (2018/C 124/01)
https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-
content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:52018XC0409(01)
4

CONCENTRATION LIMITS
APPLICABLE IN WASTE
CLASSIFICATION
If the concentration of a single substance is equal or
greater than the concentration limit specified, the
waste is classified as having the corresponding hazard
property.
Source:
Commission notice on technical guidance on the classification of
waste (2018/C 124/01)
https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-
content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:52018XC0409(01)
5

CONCENTRATION LIMITS
APPLICABLE IN WASTE
CLASSIFICATION
Source: Ministry of the Environment, Guide – Classification of Waste as Hazardous Waste, Table 3A6

CHAIN OF WASTE MANAGEMENT
FOR HAZARDOUS WASTE
Waste generation
RESPONSIBILITY
FOR THE WASTE
TRANSFERS7Classification Selecting of container
Packaging and labeling Storage
Transportation documents Transportation
Reception TreatmentBilling and
reportingProject planning and risk assesment
Waste management
company

HOW TO COLLECT HAZARDOUS WASTE
8Collect into separate containers chemicals that react together .
When collecting waste chemicals, always check that mixing the
chemicals will not produce dangerous compounds. Always handle the
mixture according to the most toxic compound
Notice! Do not mix waste chemicals if you are
not absolutely sure what you are doing
If you have even the slightest doubt, it is better to deliver
each waste chemical separately for treatment
The Waste Act (§17) prohibits the dilution or mixing of hazardous
waste with other types of waste or substances.
Wastes that differ in type and quality must be collected and kept
separate from each other .Examples of waste to be
collected separately:
- Solids and liquids
- Acids and bases always
collected separately
Remember that the corrosive
symbol does not indicate the
substance's pH.
It could be an acid (pH < 7)
or a base (pH > 7).
- Substances that react with
water (e.g., calcium oxide)
- Cyanides (keep separate
especially with acids!)
- Organic peroxides

SELECTING THE WASTE CONTAINER
Take into consideration when selecting a waste container:
- How much waste will be generated?
1 ml / 1000 ml?
- Is the material resistant to the chemical in question?
Glass / Plastic ?
What material was the original chemical package?
- The container must be airtight and resealable
- The packaging must withstand the usual handling
- The packaging must not react with the hazardous waste
in such a way that it causes danger or harm to health or
the environment
The best waste container is the original chemical packaging
Add the text "WASTE“ or “JÄTE” and remove any
unnecessary markings
If the original packaging is still in use, choose the waste
container that is made from similar material as the
original packaging
Notice!
Do not use food packaging!
Do not use storage bottles to collect waste!

WASTE LABELING
- The waste must be clearly labeled as waste (WASTE)
- The correct name of the hazardous substance must be used, not a
"nickname“ or shortname
- If a solution, the maximum concentration of the hazardous
substance must be stated.
Example: <4% / <16% / <90%
- All hazard symbols for the hazardous properties must be circled
Check the original chemical container for labeling details
- The date when the waste container was established
- It’s good practice to regularly review waste containers
and send the oldest for waste treatment,
as packaging may become brittle over time.
- The name of the waste holder
- Label templates can be printed on ordinary paper as long as the
label is covered thoroughly with clear tape to prevent the label
text from becoming unreadable due to splashes.
- Commonly used labels can be prepared in advance.
10
Notice!
If you use old chemical containers for
collecting waste chemicals, remove all
unnecessary, misleading markings!

STORAGE OF HAZARDOUS WASTE
-Hazardous waste must be stored in a well-ventilated cabinet or area.
- The general rule is that flammable liquids and gases should not be stored in
the same area as corrosive and oxidizing chemical waste.
- Large quantities of flammable liquids should be stored in fire-resistant,
partitioned cabinets or areas.
- It’s good practice to place a protective container made of polypropylene (PP)
or polyethylene (PE) under the waste container .
Notice! Highly toxic substances must not be stored in the same cabinet as
substances that could cause a fire or explosion.
In case of an explosion, the spread of the toxic substance could cause more
damage than the explosion itself.
Notice! Do not store waste in such a way that
waste and usable chemicals can mix!
11

SUPPLIES FOR THE WASTE
STORAGE
- Label stickers / Label templates (and a marker)
- Suitable collection containers for chemical waste
Nearby the waste storage:
The necessary personal protective equipment
should be available for handling waste
especially eye protection and protective gloves, as
well as a respirator
In case of an accident, absorbent material and
containers for accidentally generated waste should be
available in or near the storage area
First aid supplies should also be nearby,
especially an eye rinse bottle
12

IDENTIFYING UNIDENTIFIED WASTE
The sender is responsible for identifying the waste!
- Determine the origin of the waste and intended use
- Physical state of the waste: Liquid, solid, or paste
- Do not smell the waste!
- If the package needs to be opened, do so cautiously!
- If liquid, measure the pH of the waste:
pH < 7acidic
pH > 7alkaline
- Remember that solid substances can also be acidic or alkaline
consider carefully is it needed to dissolve in water!
dissolve in water with caution!
- Write the information into waste label13

THANK YOU!
HEINI LASSILA
